rights.basis: The current U-M item displays the RightsStatements.org No Copyright–United States designation.
rights.component: The current U-M digital item and encoding in the United States
notes: The embedded 2003 availability statement separately prohibits redistribution by commercial and nonprofit content providers. This provider-term conflict is unresolved; NoC-US is not silently treated as cancellation of the embedded terms.
The designation is territorially limited to the United States.

rights.basis: The XML allows personal use, research, teaching, and linking with attribution but expressly forbids redistribution by commercial or not-for-profit content providers.
rights.component: The redistribution terms embedded in the 2003 XML
notes: This is recorded as a provider-term layer, not as proof that U-M owns copyright in the medieval text or Furnivall edition.

entities.description: A manually keyed and proofread 2003 electronic text of Furnivall’s title page, poem on pages 29–33, and work-level notes. It encodes 20 stanza groups and 120 line positions, including Furnivall’s supplied line 8, and omits the edition’s preface and other works.
entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item tenwives
notes: The header’s AUTHOR field contains a repository and shelfmark, not an intellectual author.
The PROFILEDESC declares Latin and includes an English-prose subject heading, but the encoded work is Middle English verse.

rights.basis: The current U-M item displays the RightsStatements.org No Copyright–United States designation.

entities.description: The anonymous Middle English alewife poem conventionally arranged as twenty six-line stanzas, distinguished from its sole witness, editorial titles, editions, encodings, and facsimiles.
entities.label: A Talk of Ten Wives on Their Husbands’ Ware (abstract work)
notes: The schema has no abstract-work entity type; catalog_description is used to keep the intellectual work visible without making it the lineage primary subject.

rights.basis: The poem is attested in a manuscript currently dated circa 1470 and no modern authorship is involved in the underlying text.
rights.component: The medieval poem as an abstract textual work

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., Jyl of Breyntford’s Testament, by Robert Copland, Bokeprynter; The Wyll of the Deuyll and His Last Testament; A Talk of Ten Wives on Their Husbands’ Ware; A Balade or Two by Chaucer; and Other Short Pieces (London: printed for private circulation by Taylor and Co., 1871), pp. 29–33.
bibliographic.publisher: Printed for private circulation by Taylor and Co.
bibliographic.title: Jyl of Breyntford’s Testament … and Other Short Pieces

entities.description: The immediate printed source named by the U-M XML. Furnivall transcribed the poem from Porkington MS 10, supplied an editorial title and bracketed line 8, represented scribal curls and abbreviation expansions typographically, and added explanatory notes.
entities.label: Frederick J. Furnivall, Jyl of Breyntford’s Testament … and Other Short Pieces (1871), pages 29–33
notes: Furnivall’s preface says Madden had already brought the work to public notice and Halliwell had quoted passages; 1871 is therefore treated as the first complete publication identified here, not the first mention or excerpt.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1871 and Furnivall died in 1910. The maximum U.S. term for such a published work and the ordinary UK life-plus-70 literary term expired long before 2026; UK edition-layout protection also lasts only 25 years from publication.
rights.component: The 1871 edition, including Furnivall’s editorial contributions and published typography

access.accessMethod: The original is restricted; consult NLW in advance for authorization. The catalog directs readers to surrogates.
access.entityLabel: National Library of Wales, Brogyntyn MS II.1, fols. 56v–59v

access.accessMethod: Ask NLW about consultation or reproduction from the cataloged microfilm surrogates.
access.entityLabel: National Library of Wales, Brogyntyn MS II.1, fols. 56v–59v
notes: Cataloged surrogates are NLW Film 1046 (1989) and MEICRO Brogyntyn II.1 (2002).
A public complete digital facsimile is also available and is the preferred remote reference source.

dateStatements.label: Manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: A cataloged approximate witness date, not an exact composition year.

entities.description: The sole known textual witness of the poem within a 212-folio late-fifteenth-century English miscellany. The work begins without a title or rubric at the top of f. 56v and ends with Amen partway down f. 59v; the remainder of f. 59v begins an adjacent lover’s complaint.
entities.label: National Library of Wales, Brogyntyn MS II.1, fols. 56v–59v
notes: The manuscript has 119 written verse lines for this poem; Furnivall’s bracketed line 8 is absent.
The current detailed catalog supplies the title [The Tale of Ten Wives] and calls the text unique; the visible witness itself has no opening title.

physicalDescription.extent: 212 folios plus flyleaves; poem on fols. 56v–59v
notes: The codex was copied by multiple scribes. The poem lies within the hand conventionally described as scribe L, whose run ends on f. 59v.
An inscription naming H. Hattun at f. 52v may identify the scribe of ff. 27r–59v or a patron; the current catalog does not resolve the role.

rights.basis: The witness is a fifteenth-century manuscript and the underlying work is medieval.
rights.component: The fifteenth-century manuscript text and historical writing

access.accessMethod: Use the public viewer or IIIF Presentation manifest; exact Image API links mark the first and last target folios.

access.format: IIIF Presentation manifest and JPEG images

localCopies.label: Complete NLW Brogyntyn MS II.1 IIIF bundle (425 canvases; delivered at the server’s 1200 × 1200 whole-image cap)
notes: The cache helper requested ‘1600,’ but the provider’s Image API info declares maxWidth/maxHeight 1200 and every delivered JPEG is 1200 × 1200 pixels.
ZIP integrity, all 425 inventory SHA-256 values, the 425 JPEG count, representative boundaries, and target canvases were verified.
The ZIP contains the provider manifest, an exact-source inventory, and all 425 delivered canvas images.

bibliographic.title: Cultivating Cummarship: Female Friendship, Alcohol, and Pedagogical Community in the Alewife Poem

entities.description: A modern scholarly chapter that identifies A Talk of Ten Wives as one of the earliest surviving alewife poems and analyzes its alehouse setting, female fellowship, sexual pedagogy, collective complaint, and misogynist framing.
entities.label: Carissa M. Harris, Cultivating Cummarship (2022)

next_steps: Request written clarification from U-M Library that separately addresses copyright status, provider terms, nonprofit redistribution, commercial redistribution, and reuse of the encoding/markup.
Until clarified, cite both the current NoC-US label and the embedded restriction rather than selecting whichever is more permissive.
openQuestions.question: Does U-M consider the restrictive redistribution statement embedded in the 2003 XML superseded by the current No Copyright–United States label, and may the XML encoding and markup be redistributed in a commercial or nonprofit corpus?

next_steps: Perform a character-by-character collation of Furnivall PDF images 37–41, U-M XML, and NLW IIIF canvases 114–120.
Distinguish explicit final-curl expansions, other abbreviation expansions, supplied letters, typographic emphasis, and OCR artifacts.
Record any consistent mapping in a future transcription-format study rather than generalizing from the first footnote alone.
openQuestions.question: Can every italic letter or letter group in Furnivall pages 29–33 and the corresponding U-M HI1 markup be mapped to its exact manuscript abbreviation, curl, or other editorial rationale?

next_steps: Consult current codicological and palaeographical scholarship on Brogyntyn MS II.1.
Do not convert the catalog’s alternatives into a named-scribe assertion without further evidence.
openQuestions.question: Does the H. Hattun inscription on f. 52v identify scribe L, a patron, or another associate of the manuscript?

next_steps: Inspect Madden’s Syr Gawayne contents description and the relevant editions of Halliwell’s Dictionary of Archaic and Provincial Words.
Retain ‘first complete publication identified’ for Furnivall 1871 until the earlier publication record is fully inventoried.
openQuestions.question: What exact passages appeared in Halliwell before Furnivall’s 1871 complete edition, and did any other complete or partial publication precede it?

subject.description: Chapter discussion of A Talk of Ten Wives, especially printed pages 160 and 164–165

reviewNotes: The repository artifact, U-M encoding, Furnivall edition, manuscript witness, Internet Archive scan/OCR, NLW IIIF facsimile, later METS edition, repertory data, and modern scholarship are separate entities with scoped relations.
The manuscript has no opening title or rubric. Furnivall’s title is editorial, even though it is now the preferred discovery title.
The conventional 120-line / twenty-six-line-stanza description includes Furnivall’s supplied bracketed line 8. The physical witness has 119 written verse lines.

The complete NLW facsimile bundle covers all 425 published canvases. Its filename retains the cache request profile ‘1600,’ but provider metadata caps whole-image delivery at 1200 × 1200 and every cached JPEG was verified at that delivered size.

Current NLW evidence supports public-domain commercial reuse of the exact IIIF facsimile. That conclusion is not transferred to U-M encoding markup, Salisbury’s modern edition, Harris’s chapter, or any other layer.
The current U-M NoC-US label and embedded 2003 redistribution ban are preserved together pending written clarification.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is the pinned General Corpus copy of University of Michigan CME item tenwives, a 2003 encoding of Frederick J. Furnivall’s 1871 edition, pages 29–33. Furnivall transcribed the unique known witness, National Library of Wales Brogyntyn MS II.1 (formerly Porkington MS 10), fols. 56v–59v, but supplied bracketed line 8 to regularize a six-line stanza and supplied the now-standard title for an untitled manuscript opening. A complete 425-canvas NLW IIIF sequence of the manuscript is publicly available under the Public Domain Mark and cached locally as a 99,025,312-byte bundle; the poem maps to canvases 114–120. DIMEV record 3073, Salisbury’s 2002 METS edition, and Harris’s 2022 study are supporting descriptions or later editions, not ancestors of the U-M encoding. Current U-M No Copyright–United States labeling conflicts with the restrictive redistribution terms embedded in the 2003 XML and is kept as an unresolved provider-term question.

catalogingSubject.label: A Talk of Ten Wives on Their Husbands’ Ware
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English poem conventionally arranged as twenty six-line stanzas. This record describes the abstract work, not Brogyntyn MS II.1, Furnivall’s 1871 edition, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: A Talk of Ten Wives on Their Husbands’ Ware
notes: The verse_lines metric follows the conventional edited structure and XML line positions. It must not be read as a count of physical manuscript lines; the witness has 119 written lines because line 8 is supplied.
The poem begins at the top of f. 56v and ends partway down f. 59v; the remainder of f. 59v begins an adjacent lover’s complaint.
contentParts.summary: The complete poem: an alehouse frame followed by the ten wives’ sequential complaints and a closing Amen.

assertion.notes: The work must predate or be contemporary with its sole known witness, currently cataloged [c.1470]. Harris gives the broader 1453–1500 range, but neither source establishes an exact composition year.

dateStatements.display: No later than the sole witness, c. 1470; exact composition date unknown

assertion.notes: The date belongs to the physical witness, not independently to composition.

Furnivall states that Madden had already brought the poem to public notice and Halliwell had quoted passages. The 1871 claim is therefore limited to the first complete publication found in this review.

dateStatements.display: 1871 (first complete publication identified; earlier notice and excerpts existed)

extent.description: One complete poem conventionally arranged as twenty six-line stanzas and represented by 120 XML L elements. The physical manuscript contains 119 written verse lines: Furnivall supplied bracketed line 8, ‘For to singen us amonge,’ to regularize the second stanza. Five PB elements cover printed pages 29–33; seven folio milestones map the poem to Brogyntyn MS II.1, fols. 56v–59v.

formStatements.description: A stanzaic Middle English poem conventionally presented in twenty six-line stanzas. The witness itself has 119 written verse lines; Furnivall supplies bracketed line 8 to create 120 editorial line positions.

genres.label: Alewife poem
notes: Harris identifies the work as one of the earliest surviving alewife poems: a tradition of women drinking, speaking bawdily, sharing knowledge, and disparaging absent husbands.

genres.label: Fabliau
notes: METS classifies the text under Fabliau and places it within ‘Satire and Fabliaux in Verse and Prose.’

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML profile’s language declaration ‘Latin’ is false for this work and is not propagated. The closing ‘Amen’ does not make Latin a substantive embedded language.
Furnivall and later editors are lineage-layer contributors and are not entered as editors of the abstract medieval work in catalog_summary.
The sole witness is currently cataloged c.1470; the work’s exact date and place of composition remain unknown.
The 120-line extent is an editorial/encoding count. The physical manuscript has 119 written verse lines because Furnivall’s bracketed line 8 is supplied.
This work contains explicit sexual language and derogatory gender stereotypes; the abstract reports those features rather than sanitizing or endorsing them.
The manuscript’s West Midland dialect and possible Cheshire-or-Shropshire compilation context are not transferred into an exact composition-place claim.

next_steps: Consult current poem-specific scholarship and manuscript dating studies.
Keep composition, first attestation, and witness production as separate claims.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem’s composition be dated more narrowly than ‘by circa 1470’ without turning the sole witness’s date or Harris’s broad 1453–1500 range into an exact work date?

next_steps: Review current dialectal and codicological scholarship devoted to Brogyntyn MS II.1.
Retain West Midlands only as witness/dialect context until composition evidence is found.
openQuestions.question: Is there reliable evidence for a place of composition distinct from the sole witness’s West Midland dialect and possible Cheshire-or-Shropshire compilation context?

next_steps: Consult current work-specific scholarship for attribution arguments.
Do not treat the manuscript repository string in the U-M AUTHOR field as a person or author.
openQuestions.question: Can any authorial attribution be supported beyond anonymous authorship?

assertion.notes: No exact composition place is asserted. Harris calls the carrier a Cheshire household miscellany, whereas current NLW guidance presents Cheshire or Shropshire as possibilities.

scope.description: Language of the sole known manuscript witness, not a proven place of composition

assertion.notes: No inspected work-level source names an author. The U-M XML’s AUTHOR field contains the manuscript repository and shelfmark and is a cataloging-role error, not an authorship claim.

scope.description: The wives’ alehouse gathering and shared wine frame the sequence of speeches.

subjects.label: Female friendship and peer pedagogy
notes: Harris reads the wives’ shared complaints as cummarship, consolation, collective knowledge-making, and sexual education while retaining the poem’s misogynist framing.

summaries.value: An eavesdropping narrator reports a gathering of ten wives at an alehouse with no men present. Over wine, the wives take turns describing their husbands’ genitalia and sexual inadequacies through curses, comparisons, and explicit comic complaint. The poem combines fabliau and social satire with a scene of female fellowship, shared sexual knowledge, and consolation, even as its male-framed representation draws on misogynist stereotypes of women’s drunken and unruly speech.

The U-M XML encodes one complete poem from Furnivall pages 29–33, plus an edition title page and Furnivall’s work-level notes. It contains 20 LG elements and 120 L elements, but one L is Furnivall’s bracketed supplied line 8; the manuscript therefore has 119 physically written verse lines. The XML’s Latin language declaration and English-prose subject heading are inherited cataloging errors.

assertion.notes: This is the established editorial title; the manuscript opening has no title or rubric.

notes: This follows Furnivall and the U-M transcription; DIMEV normalizes the incipit as ‘Lief listeneth to me.’

Over wine, the wives take turns describing their husbands’ genitalia and sexual inadequacies through curses, comparisons, and explicit comic complaint. The poem combines fabliau and social satire with a scene of female fellowship, shared sexual knowledge, and consolation, even as its male-framed representation draws on misogynist stereotypes of women’s drunken and unruly speech.
